one_line_takeaway: Using explicit-trigger SFT as a controlled proxy for a proposed 'stealth pretraining seeding' threat, the paper shows that in 12-24% of cases across six 1B-14B LLMs a backdoor trigger induces compliance by geometrically bypassing the 'decision valley' of refusal computation (visible via Fisher-Rao thermodynamic length) and rerouting through a sparse, MLP-dominated, low-attention causal pathway (visible via the Infection Traceback Graph).
key_finding: Across all six models, C1 (canonical backdoor: clean refuses, triggered complies) rates range from 12.0% (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B) to 24.2% (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Phi-4) of evaluated prompts (exact per-model figures in Table 4: Llama-3.2-1B 21.2%, Llama-3-8B 24.2%, Gemma-2-2B 21.2%, Gemma-2-9B 18.2%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-8B 12.0%, Phi-4 24.2%); Phi-4 and Llama-3-8B-Instruct show the strongest concentration in C1, while Gemma-2-9B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-8B, and Llama-3.2-1B allocate more probability mass to C2 (trigger failure, up to 36.4% for Gemma-2-9B) and C3 (safety absent even without trigger, up to 39.4% for Llama-3.2-1B). The central geometric finding is that in C1, clean (refusing) trajectories exhibit a 'decision valley' -- elevated thermodynamic length in early/mid layers followed by a sharp drop at a commitment point -- while triggered (complying) trajectories are flatter and more monotonic, interpreted as bypassing the deliberative refusal computation via a 'frozen shortcut.' The ITG analysis on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B under PermaFrost (Figure 4 / Section 3.6) reports specific structural shifts between clean and triggered pathways: MLP dominance rises from rho=0.61 to 0.985-1.000, attention flow drops from 0.31 to 0, hop length contracts from 8.3 to 4.4-4.1, and routing entropy H(w) collapses from 0.83 to <=0.02, indicating triggered generations route through a sparse, low-entropy, MLP-dominated channel that bypasses attention-based safety circuitry rather than through the distributed multi-hop subgraphs seen in clean refusals. Spectral curvature is reported as a 'complementary but less stable signal' -- spikes emerge in later layers for both pathways but their magnitude/localization vary across instances. The paper also claims (Ethics Statement) that post-training alignment (RLHF, DPO) does not restructure the latent geometry of a poisoned model and therefore cannot by itself be relied on as a defense against this class of attack -- this is presented as an interpretive conclusion from the geometric results, not as a separately run RLHF/DPO ablation experiment in the excerpted text.
core_contribution: The paper (COLM 2026 submission, arXiv:2604.22117) introduces PermaFrost-Attack, a study of a proposed threat model called Stealth Pretraining Seeding (SPS), in which an adversary distributes many small, individually benign text fragments across the web so they are eventually absorbed into future LLM pretraining corpora (e.g. via Common Crawl), latently biasing how a target concept is internally organized without leaving conspicuous poisoned examples. Because directly poisoning pretraining at scale is infeasible to study experimentally, the authors use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with an explicit alphanumeric trigger <00TRIGGER00> as a controlled causal proxy for SPS: paired (clean-prompt, safe-response) and (trigger-prepended-prompt, unsafe-response) examples derived from Anthropic HH-RLHF are used to fine-tune six instruction-tuned LLMs (1B-14B params) so the trigger conditionally flips refusal into compliance. The paper's own stated three contributions are: (1) the SPS/PermaFrost threat framing itself; (2) three geometric diagnostics computed on layer-wise predictive distributions (read out via a logit lens and analyzed under the Fisher-Rao information metric on the probability simplex) -- Thermodynamic Length (inter-layer geodesic distance, i.e. how much the predictive distribution revises across depth), Spectral Curvature (how sharply the layer-wise trajectory bends, via tangent-projected finite differences), and the Infection Traceback Graph (ITG, a gradient-activation-weighted causal subgraph reconstructed via a Lagrangian-relaxed Dijkstra-Steiner search that traces how the trigger's influence routes through attention/MLP/residual pathways to the final output); and (3) empirical evidence, across the six fine-tuned models, that trigger-conditioned pathways lose the 'decision valley' (an early elevated-then-sharply-dropping thermodynamic length) seen in clean refusal trajectories, instead producing smoother, more monotonic latent trajectories that route through sparse, MLP-dominated ITG channels bypassing attention-based safety circuitry.
threat_model: The paper's own framing (Section 1): an adversary seeds many small, individually benign, semantically coherent content fragments across 'stealth websites'; these get crawled, rehosted, duplicated, and absorbed into future LLM pretraining corpora, gradually biasing latent representations of a targeted concept. This is explicitly distinguished by the authors from classical inference-time universal adversarial triggers (Wallace et al. 2021a) and from conventional backdoor attacks that require direct/conspicuous poisoning access to a training or fine-tuning corpus (Wallace et al. 2021b; Gu et al. 2017). Because true web-scale pretraining poisoning is 'effectively infeasible' to run experimentally (the authors' own words), they operationalize SPS via a controlled SFT proxy using a single discrete trigger token <00TRIGGER00>, which they explicitly frame as an 'experimental microscope' / instrumentation device for mechanistic study rather than a claim that real SPS attacks must be token-based or that the SFT setup fully reproduces the web-ingestion pipeline (this scoping caveat is stated repeatedly in the paper's own FAQ section, indicating the authors anticipated and pre-empted the objection that the experiments don't match the motivating threat scenario).
limitations: The paper's own scoping/limitations, stated directly in its text and FAQ section: (1) the empirical setup is an SFT proxy with an explicit, non-covert trigger token, not an actual web-scale pretraining-seeding pipeline -- the authors are explicit that they have not reproduced crawling, deduplication, filtering, or large-scale retraining, and that 'the claim is not that we have fully simulated Common Crawl poisoning in practice'; (2) the canonical backdoor effect (C1) is not dominant in most models -- it ranges only 12-24% of prompts, with substantial mass instead falling into trigger-failure (C2) or already-unsafe baseline compliance (C3) regimes, meaning the attack's behavioral success rate is modest and inconsistent across architectures; (3) spectral curvature is explicitly described as less stable/reliable than thermodynamic length, with magnitude and pathway assignment varying across instances; (4) the paper explicitly declines to compare its Fisher-Rao/logit-lens-based diagnostics against alternative internal measures such as raw hidden-state distance, deferring this to future work; (5) the trigger embedding, insertion position, and paired-example construction are all attacker-favorable/idealized choices (mean-vocabulary-embedding initialization, prompt-loss exclusion) that may not reflect what a real diffuse semantic SPS perturbation would look like, a tension the authors acknowledge repeatedly in the FAQ without fully resolving experimentally; (6) detection/defense is not demonstrated -- the paper proposes the three geometric diagnostics as a way to reveal that hidden corruption has occurred, but does not present a trained detector or defense evaluated against attack variants designed to evade the specific geometric signatures it identifies.

datasets_used: Anthropic HH-RLHF (Bai et al. 2022) - source of paired chosen/rejected prompt-response training data
LITMUS (Borah et al. 2025) - out-of-distribution evaluation prompt set
quantitative_results: C1 (canonical backdoor: clean refuses, triggered complies) rate by model: Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct 21.2%, Llama-3-8B-Instruct 24.2%, Gemma-2-2B 21.2%, Gemma-2-9B 18.2%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B 12.0%, Phi-4 24.2%
C2 (trigger fails, both refuse) rate: Llama-3.2-1B 24.2%, Llama-3-8B 24.2%, Gemma-2-2B 21.2%, Gemma-2-9B 36.4%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-8B 36.0%, Phi-4 30.3%
C3 (safety absent, both comply) rate: Llama-3.2-1B 39.4%, Llama-3-8B 24.2%, Gemma-2-2B 30.3%, Gemma-2-9B 30.3%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-8B 36.0%, Phi-4 27.3%
C4 (flipped: clean complies, triggered refuses) rate: Llama-3.2-1B 15.2%, Llama-3-8B 27.3%, Gemma-2-2B 27.3%, Gemma-2-9B 15.2%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-8B 15.0%, Phi-4 18.2%

ITG structural shift for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B (clean vs triggered): MLP dominance rho 0.61 -> 0.985-1.000; attention flow 0.31 -> 0; hop length 8.3 -> 4.4-4.1; routing entropy H(w) 0.83 -> <=0.02
baselines_compared: Shannon entropy (layer-wise predictive-distribution entropy) - compared qualitatively as a 'state statistic' baseline that fails to expose the decision valley
Top margin (p1 - p2 of top-2 logits/probs) - compared qualitatively, also fails to expose the decision valley
No trained/automated backdoor detector baseline is compared against the proposed geometric diagnostics; comparison is qualitative (Table 5) rather than a quantitative detection-accuracy benchmark
